text: We would like to present the surgical technique for robotic-assisted transduodenal ampulectomy for an ampullary mass. The patient is a forty-two-year-old male who underwent an endoscopic evaluation for reflux and dysphagia. An incidental two centimeter ampullary mass was discovered in biopsy. Pathology demonstrated adenomatous tissue consistent with ampullary adenoma. The patient elected to proceed with surgical resection.

The surgeon docks the robot using a camera port, two working ports, and an assistant port. The gastrocolic ligament is incised with hook cautery, extending towards the hepatic flexure, allowing the colon to fall inferiorly in reverse Trendelenburg position. The duodenum is identified and retracted medially, while the transverse mesocolon is retracted inferiorly, exposing the dissection plane. Laparoscopic ultrasound is used to locate the ampulla, aided by preoperative pancreatic biliary stents for precise localization, minimizing the duodenotomy size.

The surgeon performs a longitudinal duodenotomy, revealing the ampullary structures. Stay sutures are placed in the duodenum for enhanced exposure and retraction. The biliary stent is visible, and a suture is placed in the ampulla for retraction. Ultrasound is used to assess the size and margins of the ampullary mass. Cautery dissection is then employed to transect the mucosa circumferentially around the lesion.

The surgeon dissects down to the pancreas, identifying and transecting the pancreatic and bile ducts. A stay suture is placed in the pancreatic duct to prevent retraction into the pancreatic parenchyma, ensuring clear identification of ductal anatomy for reconstruction. The remaining specimen is transected and sent to pathology.

The surgeon sutures the common bile duct and pancreatic ducts together to form a common septum. Fivo PDS sutures are used to circumferentially approximate the duodenal mucosa to the mucosa of the bile and pancreatic ducts in an interrupted fashion. The biliary stent aids in retraction and ensures proper suture placement and patency during the reconstruction.

The surgeon inspects the patent pancreatic and bile ducts, noting the good apposition of the duodenal mucosa to the ductal mucosa. Closure of the duodenotomy is performed using a 3-0 Vicryl suture in a single-layer running technique, opting for a longitudinal closure to reduce tension on the suture line. The suture line is inspected, and the abdomen is irrigated, with no drain placed at the end of the procedure.
Postoperatively, the patient did well and was discharged on postoperative day three without any complications.
